mnemonics.title: TCP Features
mnemonics.content: SCAF RUH: Sequence numbers, Congestion control, Acknowledgments, Flow control, Reliability, UDP (no flow control), Headers (20-60 bytes)

mnemonics.title: Three-Way Handshake
mnemonics.content: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK: 'SYN' sounds like 'SIN' - first step is requesting connection; 'SYN-ACK' is server's agreement; final 'ACK' is client's confirmation like acknowledging a deal.

mnemonics.title: Flow vs Congestion Control
mnemonics.content: Flow: 'F' in Flow = F for Final destination (Receiver). Congestion: 'C' = C for Congestion in the network path (routers).

acronyms.usage: Reliable, connection-oriented protocol for ordered data delivery with flow control.
Connectionless protocol for fast, unreliable data transfer without flow control.
Largest TCP payload size negotiated during connection setup.
TCP field indicating receiver's available buffer space for flow control.
Message sent by receiver to confirm receipt of data segments.
TCP flag used to initiate a connection during three-way handshake.
Error control mechanism that automatically retransmits lost packets.
Algorithm to reduce small packet transmission overhead in TCP.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a rectangular window sliding over a row of numbered boxes. The window covers boxes that can be sent. As ACKs arrive, the window slides right, revealing new boxes to send.
Draw a 20-byte box divided into sections: Ports (2 boxes), Seq/Ack numbers (4 boxes), Flags/Window (2 boxes), Checksum/Urgent (2 boxes). Add extra 40 bytes for options.
Person A (client) extends hand (SYN) → Person B (server) shakes and holds (SYN-ACK) → Person A completes handshake (ACK). Connection established.
Traffic light analogy: Red light (Window=0) = STOP sending. Green light (Window>0) = GO. Sender checks periodically.
Go-Back-N: Like rewinding an entire cassette tape when one song skips. Selective Repeat: Like only replaying the skipped song on a playlist.

analogies.analogy: Like a waiter filling water glasses: The waiter (sender) watches the guest's glass (receiver). When glass is full, waiter stops pouring until guest drinks (processes data) and shows empty glass again.

UDP is like sending a postcard - cheap, fast, but no guarantee it arrives or arrives in order. TCP is like registered mail with return receipt - more overhead but guaranteed delivery and confirmation.
Think of a bus with a fixed number of seats (window size). New passengers (packets) can only board when previous passengers show their tickets (ACKs) and seats become available.
Flow control is one-on-one conversation where you wait for the other person to nod before continuing. Congestion control is like traffic lights managing an entire intersection to prevent gridlock.
Like taking turns speaking in a meeting: Person A speaks, waits for nod/acknowledgment, then Person B speaks. Very orderly but slow.
